record_office_terminology.land_records: Chancery Clerk (deed books, mortgages, liens, trust deeds -- one per county)
record_office_terminology.probate: Chancery Court / Chancery Clerk (wills, estates, guardianships -- Mississippi uses Chancery Court for probate, a unique system with no separate probate court)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Chancery Clerk (marriage licenses and bonds from county formation; Chancery Clerk is the issuing authority)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1912 (statewide birth and death registration began; compliance improved through the 1920s; earlier vital events found in church records, census, and county records; Mississippi State Department of Health is the state registrar)

burned_county_authority: Mississippi Department of Archives and History (MDAH), Jackson -- central repository for Mississippi records. Mississippi has approximately 20 burned or significantly damaged counties from courthouse fires, Civil War destruction, and Gulf Coast hurricane damage. MDAH holds microfilm of surviving county records, and researchers should also consult BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), census records, Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA, and church records to supplement destroyed civil records.
administrative_notes: Mississippi Territory was established in 1798 from the Natchez District (formerly under French, British, and Spanish sovereignty). Statehood was achieved on December 10, 1817, as the 20th state. Mississippi has 82 counties. The state's unique legal system uses Chancery Court for probate matters -- there is no separate probate court. The Chancery Clerk handles land records (deeds), marriage licenses, and chancery court (probate/equity) matters. The Circuit Clerk handles criminal and civil court records. Mississippi is a public land state -- federal land patents from the Bureau of Land Management General Land Office (glorecords.blm.gov) are essential for documenting original land entries, especially in Choctaw Cession (Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek, 1830) and Chickasaw Cession (Treaty of Pontotoc, 1832) lands that comprise most of central and northern Mississippi. The Natchez District counties (Adams, Jefferson, Claiborne, Wilkinson) have the oldest records, dating from the territorial period and including French, Spanish, and British colonial documents. Mississippi seceded from the Union in January 1861, and Civil War destruction (particularly the Siege of Vicksburg, Sherman's raids, and Union cavalry operations) caused significant record losses in multiple counties. The Mississippi Department of Archives and History (MDAH) in Jackson is the central repository and has microfilmed county records extensively. The Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians maintains a reservation in east-central Mississippi, and tribal records are held at NARA (Record Group 75) and the Choctaw tribal archives. Freedmen's Bureau field offices operated across Mississippi and their records at NARA (M826, M1907) are essential for African American genealogical research.

core_repositories.source_name: Mississippi Department of Archives and History (MDAH)
core_repositories.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
core_repositories.description: Mississippi's state archives and the single most important genealogical repository for the state. Holds microfilm of county records (deeds, chancery court, marriage, estate files) from all 82 counties. Collections include territorial and colonial-era documents, Confederate service records, plantation records, slave records, state government records, private manuscripts, maps, and photographs. The Mississippi Provincial Archives contain copies of French, Spanish, and British colonial records. Research room open to the public in Jackson.

core_repositories.repository: National Archives at Atlanta / NARA, Washington DC
core_repositories.description: Federal records for Mississippi including census records, Freedmen's Bureau records (M826, M1907), military service and pension records, federal court records, BLM GLO land patent records, and Indian Affairs records (Record Group 75) for the Choctaw and Chickasaw Nations. Essential for Civil War, African American, and Native American research.

core_repositories.source_name: Mississippi State Department of Health -- Vital Records
core_repositories.repository: Mississippi State Department of Health, Jackson
core_repositories.description: State registrar for Mississippi vital records. Birth records from 1912 (some earlier), death records from 1912, and marriage records from 1926 at the state level. County-level marriage records are earlier and held by Chancery Clerks. Historical records may have been transferred to MDAH.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Mississippi county records (deeds, chancery court, marriages), vital records, church records, and cemetery records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at MDAH and county chancery clerk offices. Key collections include Mississippi marriages, chancery court records, and county deed records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability guides, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Mississippi's unique chancery court probate system, burned counties, and cession land research strategies.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Mississippi newspapers. Includes papers from Natchez, Jackson, Vicksburg, Meridian, and other cities. Legal notices, obituaries, estate advertisements, slave sale advertisements, and community news. The Mississippi Digital Library also hosts some digitized newspapers.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Microfilmed Mississippi newspapers held at MDAH. Coverage includes county weeklies and city dailies from across the state. Researchers should check newspapers for legal notices, estate settlements, runaway slave advertisements, and vital event announcements predating civil registration.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Mississippi Enslaved Persons and Plantation Records -- MDAH
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Mississippi was one of the largest slaveholding states. MDAH holds extensive plantation records, estate inventories listing enslaved persons by name and value, slave schedules (1850, 1860), manumission records, slave sale bills, and slaveholder correspondence. The collection covers all 82 counties with particular depth in Delta and Natchez District counties where enslaved populations were largest.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Freedmen's Bureau -- Mississippi Field Office Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Freedmen's Bureau records for Mississippi (NARA M826 for the Assistant Commissioner, M1907 for field office records) document formerly enslaved families through labor contracts, marriage registrations, complaints, school records, and ration distributions. Field offices operated across Mississippi from 1865-1872. FamilySearch has digitized and indexed many of these records.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Choctaw Removal Records -- NARA Record Group 75
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Records of the Bureau of Indian Affairs (Record Group 75) documenting the Choctaw Nation removal following the Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830). Includes removal rolls, allotment records, the Armstrong Roll (1831), census rolls, and agency correspondence. Essential for tracing Choctaw families who removed to Indian Territory or remained in Mississippi. The Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians (MBCI) tribal archives also hold records.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Chickasaw Removal Records -- NARA Record Group 75
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Records of the Bureau of Indian Affairs (Record Group 75) documenting the Chickasaw Nation removal following the Treaty of Pontotoc (1832). Includes removal rolls, allotment records, census rolls, and agency correspondence. The Chickasaw sold their lands individually (unlike the Choctaw communal cession), creating unique land records.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: BLM GLO Federal Land Patents -- Mississippi
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Mississippi is a public land state. Federal land patents for all Mississippi counties are searchable and downloadable at glorecords.blm.gov. Particularly important for Choctaw Cession (post-1830) and Chickasaw Cession (post-1832) lands in central and northern Mississippi, where original land entries document the first legal landowners after Native American removal.

military_records.source_name: Mississippi Confederate Service Records -- MDAH / NARA / Fold3
military_records.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History / NARA / Fold3
military_records.description: Mississippi contributed approximately 80,000 soldiers to the Confederate army. MDAH holds state adjutant general records and the Compiled Service Records of Confederate Soldiers from Mississippi. NARA has the original compiled service records. Fold3 has digitized many. Also includes pension records (Mississippi granted Confederate pensions from 1888).

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- Mississippi
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Mississippi soldiers (both Confederate and Union). Includes US Colored Troops recruited from Mississippi's African American population.

land_and_property.source_name: Mississippi Land Records -- Chancery Clerks / MDAH
land_and_property.repository: County Chancery Clerk offices / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
land_and_property.description: Each of Mississippi's 82 counties has a Chancery Clerk who maintains deed books, trust deeds, and mortgage records. MDAH holds microfilm copies of historical deed books from all counties. Mississippi is a public land state -- original federal land patents from the BLM GLO precede county deed records. Spanish, British, and French colonial land grants in the Natchez District predate the federal land system.

vital_records.source_name: Mississippi Vital Records -- State Department of Health / MDAH
vital_records.repository: Mississippi State Department of Health, Jackson / MDAH
vital_records.description: Statewide birth and death registration began in 1912, with improving compliance through the 1920s. Marriage records at the state level from 1926 (county-level marriage records are earlier, held by Chancery Clerks). For pre-1912 vital events, researchers must use church records, cemetery records, census records, and newspaper notices. Historical records may be available at MDAH.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Mississippi Church Records -- MDAH / Local Congregations
church_and_cemetery.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson / Local congregations
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are essential for pre-civil registration research in Mississippi (before 1912). Baptist and Methodist congregations predominate throughout the state. Catholic church records are important on the Gulf Coast (French/Spanish colonial heritage). Presbyterian, Episcopal, and other denominations have records from the early 1800s in the Natchez District. MDAH holds microfilm of some church records, but many remain with local congregations.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Mississippi. Growing coverage across all 82 counties. The Mississippi Genealogical Society and county-level societies have published cemetery transcription volumes. Particularly valuable in burned counties where civil records were destroyed.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society publishing the Mississippi Genealogical Exchange. Maintains surname files, research guides, and county-level resources. Affiliated with county genealogical and historical societies across the state.

Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all 82 Mississippi counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, and county histories. Valuable for burned county research where original records were destroyed.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Mississippi has no fully extinct counties, but several early counties were renamed or reorganized. Pickering County (1799) was renamed Jefferson County in 1802. Colfax County (1871) was renamed Clay County in 1876. Sumner County (1874) was renamed Webster County in 1882. The original vast Washington County (created 1809) was progressively subdivided into numerous counties; the modern Washington County (formed 1827) is much smaller. Mississippi originally had only two counties -- Adams and Pickering (Jefferson) -- in 1799, covering the Natchez District. The state expanded through the Choctaw Cession of 1820, the Choctaw Cession of 1830 (Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek), and the Chickasaw Cession of 1832 (Treaty of Pontotoc), which opened central and northern Mississippi for white settlement. The Alabama boundary was established in 1817 at statehood. The final county (Humphreys) was formed in 1918.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Mississippi has approximately 20 burned or significantly damaged counties from courthouse fires, Civil War destruction, and Gulf Coast hurricane damage. The Civil War caused the most widespread damage, particularly during the Vicksburg Campaign, Sherman's Meridian Expedition, and Union cavalry raids in north Mississippi. Gulf Coast counties (Hancock, Harrison, Jackson) have suffered repeated hurricane damage, most recently from Katrina (2005). Courthouse fires in the late 1800s destroyed records in several counties. MDAH has microfilmed surviving records from all counties and is the first stop for burned county research. BLM GLO federal land patents, census records, Freedmen's Bureau records, and church records are the primary substitutes for destroyed county records.
